Home
Ph.D. Candidate in Philosophy · Boston College

Maria
Gallego-Ortiz

Imagination, ethics, and the moral life — between phenomenology and the Aristotelian tradition.

Stokes Hall · Boston College
About

I am a philosopher working at the intersection of moral philosophy, phenomenology, and the imagination. My research asks how the imagination shapes moral perception — how we see other people, how we attend to the world, and how attention itself becomes an ethical practice.

I am currently a Ph.D. candidate in Philosophy at Boston College, where I work primarily on Iris Murdoch, Paul Ricœur, and Gaston Bachelard. Before Boston, I read philosophy at the University of St Andrews and at the Universidad de La Sabana in Bogotá, where I am from.

Alongside my research, I teach Philosophy of the Person at Boston College, and I convene the Boston Phenomenology Circle. I write, on occasion, on questions of hospitality, embodiment, and literature.

    Bachelard Studies

    Philosophies 11(2), 43

    Continental Philosophy Review 58, 403–423

Maria Gallego-Ortiz · Chestnut Hill, MAMMXXVI
Research
Research

On imagination and
the moral life

Statement

My dissertation develops an account of moral imagination drawing on Iris Murdoch's notion of attention and Paul Ricœur's hermeneutics of the self. I argue that imagination is not an ornament to ethical thought but its primary medium — the faculty by which the moral world becomes visible to us as a world of particular others.

Areas
  • —Moral imagination and ethical perception
  • —Phenomenology of embodiment and hospitality
  • —Iris Murdoch and twentieth-century moral realism
  • —Ricœur, Bachelard, and the poetics of selfhood
  • —Aristotle on phantasia and the rational soul
  • —Feminist phenomenology and disability studies
In progress

Imagination as Moral Vision

Dissertation, in progress

A study of attention, imagination, and particularity in the work of Iris Murdoch, with detours through Ricœur and Bachelard.

The Hospitality of the Pregnant Body

Article project

Continuing the line of thought begun in Continental Philosophy Review (2025).

Maria Gallego-Ortiz · Chestnut Hill, MAMMXXVI

Maria Gallego-Ortiz · Chestnut Hill, MAMMXXVI
Teaching
Teaching

A practice of attention

Approach

I have been teaching philosophy in one form or another since 2015 — first to high-school students in Bogotá, then to undergraduates at La Sabana, and now to first-year students at Boston College. I think of teaching less as the transfer of knowledge than as a shared practice of attention.

On the work
of imagination.

Imagination, ethics, and the moral life — between phenomenology and the Aristotelian tradition.

About

I am a philosopher working at the intersection of moral philosophy, phenomenology, and the imagination. My research asks how the imagination shapes moral perception — how we see other people, how we attend to the world, and how attention itself becomes an ethical practice.

I am currently a Ph.D. candidate in Philosophy at Boston College, where I work primarily on Iris Murdoch, Paul Ricœur, and Gaston Bachelard. Before Boston, I read philosophy at the University of St Andrews and at the Universidad de La Sabana in Bogotá, where I am from.

Alongside my research, I teach Philosophy of the Person at Boston College, and I convene the Boston Phenomenology Circle. I write, on occasion, on questions of hospitality, embodiment, and literature.
